question: [File: 'File Name: web3/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/web3/throttle/ThrottleManagerImpl.java -> Scope: High: Temporary freezing of network transactions by delaying one block by 500% or more of the average block time of the preceding 24 hours beyond standard difficulty adjustments'] [Function: throttle()] Can an unprivileged external user exhaust the shared `gasLimitBucket` by submitting a single `ContractCallRequest` with `gas` set to the maximum allowed value (up to `maxGasLimit`), consuming all available tokens in one call, causing all subsequent legitimate transactions to receive `GAS_PER_SECOND_LIMIT_EXCEEDED` and be frozen for the remainder of the refill window?

[File: 'File Name: web3/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/web3/throttle/ThrottleManagerImpl.java -> Scope: High: Temporary freezing of network transactions by delaying one block by 500% or more of the average block time of the preceding 24 hours beyond standard difficulty adjustments'] [Function: throttle()] Can an unprivileged external user send a burst of `ContractCallRequest` calls each with gas just above `GAS_SCALE_FACTOR` (10,000) to drain the `gasLimitBucket` incrementally within a single second, causing the network to reject all subsequent transactions with `GAS_PER_SECOND_LIMIT_EXCEEDED` for the full 1-second refill window, effectively freezing block processing?

[File: 'File Name: web3/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/web3/throttle/ThrottleManagerImpl.java -> Scope: High: Temporary freezing of network transactions by delaying one block by 500% or more of the average block time of the preceding 24 hours beyond standard difficulty adjustments'] [Function: throttle()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the fact that `rateLimitBucket.tryConsume(1)` is checked before `gasLimitBucket.tryConsume()` by sending exactly `requestsPerSecond` (500) requests with minimal gas to exhaust the rate bucket, then sending high-gas requests from a second IP/connection to drain the gas bucket simultaneously, causing a compound freeze of all transaction types?

[File: 'File Name: web3/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/web3/throttle/ThrottleManagerImpl.java -> Scope: High: Temporary freezing of network transactions by delaying one block by 500% or more of the average block time of the preceding 24 hours beyond standard difficulty adjustments'] [Function: scaleGas()] Can an unprivileged external user submit a `ContractCallRequest` with `gas` set to exactly `GAS_SCALE_FACTOR` (10,000) or below, causing `scaleGas()` to return `0`, which makes `gasLimitBucket.tryConsume(0)` always succeed without consuming any tokens, allowing the attacker to bypass gas throttling entirely and flood the system with zero-cost gas requests that still consume rate-limit tokens and CPU, degrading throughput for legitimate users?

[File: 'File Name: web3/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/web3/throttle/ThrottleManagerImpl.java -> Scope: High: Temporary freezing of network transactions by delaying one block by 500% or more of the average block time of the preceding 24 hours beyond standard difficulty adjustments'] [Function: restore()] Can an unprivileged external user trigger a scenario where `restore(long gas)` is called with a very large `gas` value (e.g., via a failed high-gas transaction that returns gas to the bucket), causing `gasLimitBucket.addTokens()` to overflow the bucket capacity and allow subsequent attackers to consume far more gas tokens than the per-second limit, effectively disabling the gas throttle and enabling a block-delay attack?

[File: 'File Name: web3/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/web3/throttle/ThrottleManagerImpl.java -> Scope: High: Temporary freezing of network transactions by delaying one block by 500% or more of the average block time of the preceding 24 hours beyond standard difficulty adjustments'] [Function: throttle()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the sequential check order in `throttle()` — where `rateLimitBucket` is checked first and `gasLimitBucket` second — by sending exactly `requestsPerSecond - 1` low-gas requests to keep the rate bucket near-empty, then sending one maximum-gas request to drain the gas bucket, causing all subsequent requests to fail on gas throttle even after the rate bucket refills, extending the effective freeze window beyond one second?

[File: 'File Name: web3/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/web3/throttle/ThrottleManagerImpl.java -> Scope: High: Temporary freezing of network transactions by delaying one block by 500% or more of the average block time of the preceding 24 hours beyond standard difficulty adjustments'] [Function: throttleOpcodeRequest()] Can an unprivileged external user exhaust the `opcodeRateLimitBucket` (default capacity 1 per second) by sending a single opcode request, causing all subsequent opcode-based transaction queries to be frozen for the entire 1-second refill window, which when repeated continuously creates a sustained denial-of-service that delays block-related opcode processing by 500% or more of normal block time?

[File: 'File Name: web3/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/web3/throttle/ThrottleManagerImpl.java -> Scope: High: Temporary freezing of network transactions by delaying one block by 500% or more of the average block time of the preceding 24 hours beyond standard difficulty adjustments'] [Function: throttle()] Can an unprivileged external user craft a `ContractCallRequest` with a `gas` value just above `GAS_SCALE_FACTOR` (e.g., 10,001) so that `scaleGas()` returns 1 token, allowing the attacker to send up to `gasPerSecond / GAS_SCALE_FACTOR` requests per second without triggering gas throttle, effectively bypassing the intended gas-per-second limit and flooding the system with cheap requests that delay block processing?

[File: 'File Name: web3/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/web3/throttle/ThrottleManagerImpl.java -> Scope: High: Temporary freezing of network transactions by delaying one block by 500% or more of the average block time of the preceding 24 hours beyond standard difficulty adjustments'] [Function: action()] Can an unprivileged external user craft a `ContractCallRequest` that matches a `RequestProperties` filter configured with `ActionType.THROTTLE`, then flood requests to exhaust the per-filter `Bucket` (created with `rate` as capacity), causing all matching legitimate transactions to be throttled and frozen while the attacker's own requests that do not match the filter continue unimpeded?
